label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking if following these instructions will make me return to the starting point. The instructions are: always face forward. Take 2 steps backward, then 5 steps backward, then 5 steps right, then 9 steps right. The options are yes or no. First, I need to visualize this. Let me imagine I'm facing forward. So, if I take steps backward, that would be in the direction opposite to where I'm facing. So 2 steps backward and then 5 more steps backward. So total of 7 steps backward. Then, taking 5 steps to the right. Since I'm still facing forward, right would be to my right side. So moving to the right while facing forward. Then 9 more steps to the right. So total of 14 steps to the right. Now, to determine if I end up back at the starting point, I need to see if the total displacement is zero. That is, the net movement in the forward/backward direction and the right/left direction should both be zero. In the forward/backward direction: I took a total of 7 steps backward. So my net movement in that direction is -7 steps (assuming forward is positive). In the right/left direction: I took a total of 14 steps to the right. So my net movement in that direction is +14 steps (right is positive). Since neither of these are zero, my total displacement is not zero. I am 7 steps behind and 14 steps to the right of my starting point. Therefore, I do not return to the starting point. Wait, but let me double check. The instructions say "always face forward." So my orientation doesn't change during the process. So taking steps backward means moving away from my initial facing direction. Taking steps right means moving to my right while facing forward. Yes, so my final position is 7 steps behind and 14 steps
